reasons.code_quality: Clean, idiomatic React/TypeScript. Good separation of concerns: email validator extracted outside the component, FormErrors interface clearly typed, controlled inputs with proper htmlFor/id pairing, noValidate prevents double-validation with browser, and role='alert' on error paragraphs shows accessibility awareness. Minor deduction: inputs lack aria-invalid and aria-describedby linkage to their error elements, which would complete the accessibility pattern.
reasons.completeness: Every acceptance criterion is satisfied: correct file path app/profile/edit/page.tsx, default export, name and email inputs present, email regex validation function present, success message is exactly 'Profile updated', 'use client' directive enables client-side state. Scope boundaries fully respected — package.json, next.config.js, and lib/supabase.ts are untouched, no new packages added, no auth or API calls introduced.
reasons.edge_cases: Covers the meaningful edge cases for this scope: empty name, name under 2 chars, whitespace-only name (via trim before length check), empty email, malformed email, success flag reset on re-submission, and error state cleared on valid submit. Functional gaps are minor: stored name state retains leading/trailing whitespace (benign without a backend), aria-invalid is not toggled on inputs when errors exist (UX/a11y gap), and the regex can pass some technically malformed addresses, but is standard for client-side validation.
reasons.security: No XSS risk — React escapes interpolated values by default and dangerouslySetInnerHTML is not used. No backend calls means no SQL injection surface. No hardcoded secrets, no eval, no unsafe patterns. The email regex validates format without sanitizing, which is appropriate for a client-side-only task. One point deducted for the theoretical false-positive surface of the basic email regex in a stricter context, though it is completely acceptable here given the explicit no-backend scope.

overall_notes: Tight, well-executed submission that meets every stated requirement without overstepping scope boundaries. The implementation is minimal in the right way — single file, no extra dependencies, clear state model. The main improvement opportunity is rounding out the accessibility pairing (aria-invalid, aria-describedby) between inputs and their error messages, which would elevate this to exemplary.
